metadata.title: Assemble a Research Packet with Source Traceability

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: What convinced me is that traceability turns a reading task into a verification task, and that removing competitor_b.md cleanly contracted the packet from 31 to 22 claims with zero orphans and a CONFLICTING SOURCES flag on the market-share disagreement. I want to stress-test that contract on my own docs. Help me: (1) assemble a packet from my sources with a source tag on every claim and a per-document claim-count index; (2) remove one source and confirm the packet contracts with no orphaned claims; (3) surface every place two sources contradict each other as an explicit conflict flag.

props.command: What convinced me is that traceability turns a reading task into a verification task, and that removing competitor_b.md cleanly contracted the packet from 31 to 22 claims with zero orphans and a CONFLICTING SOURCES flag on the market-share disagreement. I want to stress-test that contract on my own docs. Help me: (1) assemble a packet from my sources with a source tag on every claim and a per-document claim-count index; (2) remove one source and confirm the packet contracts with no orphaned claims; (3) surface every place two sources contradict each other as an explicit conflict flag.
props.segment: Assemble a Research Packet with Source

beats.narration_text: Source traceability converts a research packet from a reading task into a verification task.

The untraceable research packet is worse than no packet — it creates the appearance of authority without the substance. Every claim that can't be traced back to a source is a claim that has to be re-researched before acting on it. The source index is not documentation; it's what makes the packet usable.
Assemble a competitive analysis from these source documents. For every claim you include, append a source tag. At the end, produce a source index showing how many claims came from each document.

props.command: claude "Assemble a competitive analysis from these source documents. For every claim you include, append a [source: filename, section] tag. At the end, produce a source index showing how many claims came from each document." < sources/*.md

beats.narration_text: The research packet script assembles from 5 source docs, tags every claim with its source, and produces a source index with claim counts per document.

Packet assembled: 31 claims, all tagged. Source index: competitor_a.md contributed 12, competitor_b.md 9, market_report.md 7, press_releases.md 3. No claim appears without a source tag. Two claims flagged: source documents contradict each other on market share figures — CONFLICTING SOURCES flag raised.
Remove one source document and re-run; verify the packet contracts and no orphaned claims appear.

props.command: python3 research_packet.py --sources competitor_a.md market_report.md press_releases.md --output packet_v2.md

beats.narration_text: After removing competitor_b.md: packet contracts from 31 to 22 claims. Source index updates: competitor_b.md no longer appears. Zero claims from the removed source appear in the packet. The packet is smaller but fully traceable to the remaining sources. Removing a source is safe — the traceability layer enforces the contract.

Every tagged claim is a claim that can be verified in one step. The source index shows which documents are doing the most work — which tells you which sources to prioritize updating. Traceability is what makes the AI's research packet a starting point rather than a trust exercise.
Next: the plan approval gate — five questions that separate delegation from abdication.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. nikbearbrown.com.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Assemble a Research Packet with Source Traceability] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
